title.en: ChatGPT Enterprise & Team: Rollout and Governance

description.en: Plan a workforce ChatGPT deployment: what Enterprise and Team plans actually provide, the governance decisions to make before seat one, and a phased cohort rollout with measurable gates.

story_variants.en:
## The Deployment That Already Happened

Here is the uncomfortable truth every CIO eventually confronts: by the time the company evaluates ChatGPT, employees are already using it. Personal accounts, personal devices, company data pasted into consumer chat — **shadow AI** is the default state, not a risk scenario. A workforce deployment of ChatGPT Enterprise (or its competitors) is therefore less about *introducing* AI and more about *replacing ungoverned usage with governed usage*. Framing it that way changes every decision downstream.

## What the Business Plans Actually Buy

OpenAI's workforce lineup mirrors what Anthropic and Google offer, which makes comparison shopping straightforward:

- **ChatGPT Team** — for small groups: a shared workspace, higher usage limits than consumer plans, and the critical governance baseline that **business data is excluded from model training by default**.
- **ChatGPT Enterprise** — the full corporate product: SAML SSO and SCIM user provisioning, domain verification, an admin console with role management, usage analytics, compliance-oriented audit APIs, longer context limits, and contractual commitments (DPA, security certifications) that let a security team say yes.

Across vendors, the enterprise tier of a chat product is really three purchases in one: **identity integration** (your IdP controls access), **data governance** (training opt-out by default, retention controls, audit visibility), and **admin tooling** (who can use what, including which connectors and custom GPT-style extensions are allowed). Claude for Enterprise and Gemini for Workspace bundle the same trio — the differentiation is in ecosystem fit, not checklist length.

> **Callout:** "No training on our data by default" is a *plan-tier* property, not a brand property. The consumer tiers of every vendor have different defaults than the business tiers. This single fact is the strongest argument for buying seats instead of tolerating shadow usage.

## Decisions Before Seat One

Governance retrofitted after rollout is ten times harder. Decide these first:

1. **Acceptable-use policy** — which data classes may be pasted or uploaded (public, internal, confidential, regulated), with concrete examples per class.
2. **Connector and extension policy** — which integrations, plugins and custom GPT-style apps are allowed; who approves new ones.
3. **Identity design** — SSO enforced, provisioning via SCIM, offboarding tested (the leaver's chat history and access must follow your JML process).
4. **Monitoring posture** — what admins may see, and what employees are *told* admins may see. Trust collapses when surveillance is discovered rather than disclosed.
5. **Success metrics** — activation rate, weekly active usage, self-reported time savings, and a harvest of use cases worth productizing via API.

## The Phased Cohort Rollout

Big-bang rollouts fail quietly: licenses purchased, dashboards dead. The pattern that works is phased cohorts with gates:

| Phase | Cohort | Purpose | Gate to proceed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pilot | 20-50 volunteers, mixed roles | Find sharp edges, seed champions | Policy violations triaged; activation above target |
| Function wave | 1-2 departments (often engineering or support) | Prove departmental value | Measured usage + documented wins |
| Company-wide | Everyone remaining | Scale what works | Ongoing metrics, quarterly review |

Two underrated ingredients: **champions** (the pilot's enthusiastic users become the internal help desk and marketing engine — a champion per team beats any training video) and **training that is role-specific** ("prompting for recruiters" lands; "introduction to LLMs" does not).

> **Adoption pitfall:** Buying seats for everyone on day one to "send a signal." The signal sent is usually an invoice. Utilization data from phased cohorts also gives you *negotiating leverage* at renewal — you know exactly how many seats are alive.

## Measuring Beyond the Login

Activation is table stakes; value is harder. Mature programs track a simple funnel: **provisioned → activated → weekly-active → habitual (daily) → amplifier** (users who build shared prompts/GPT-style apps for teammates). Each stage transition is a coaching opportunity, not just a metric. And the most valuable output of a workforce rollout is often *discovery*: the recurring use cases employees invent are your roadmap for API-based automation — a chat product is, among other things, a paid focus group for what to build properly.

One balanced note: seat-based chat products from all three vendors share a weakness — cost is fixed while usage is wildly skewed. A minority of power users typically generates most of the value. That is normal; manage it by reallocating dormant seats quarterly rather than by declaring failure.

## What You'll Build

The challenge distills rollout planning to its arithmetic core: a phase planner that walks cohorts in order, tracks cumulative seats, and reports the final count — the same running-total logic behind every rollout dashboard and license negotiation you will ever run. Deterministic, auditable, and a nice reminder that governance programs ultimately reduce to numbers someone must get exactly right.

description.en: A workforce rollout replaces ungoverned shadow usage with identity-bound, policy-governed, measured usage.

left.label: Buy seats for everyone on day one to send a signal

right.label: Phased cohorts with gates: pilot, function wave, company-wide

description.en: Big-bang rollouts produce dead dashboards; gated cohorts produce champions, evidence and renewal leverage.

animation_sequence.code:
phases = [("pilot", 25), ("engineering", 150)]
total = 0
for name, seats in phases:
    total += seats
    print(f"{name}: {seats} seats (total {total})")

explanation.en: Each phase is a cohort with a seat count — the rollout plan as data.
A running total tracks cumulative provisioned seats — the number finance and the vendor both watch.
Per-phase reporting makes the plan auditable at every gate.

description.en: A three-phase seat plan reaching full deployment — with the cumulative math every license negotiation runs on.
The adoption funnel that matters after login: each stage transition is a coaching opportunity.

instruction.en: Build a rollout seat planner. Complete plan_rollout(phases): iterate the (name, seats) tuples in order, keep a cumulative total, and return a list of strings '<name>: <seats> seats (total <cumulative>)' — one per phase. Then print EXACTLY four lines: the three phase lines from plan_rollout(PHASES) in order, followed by 'rollout_complete=' plus the final cumulative seat count. This running-total arithmetic is the backbone of every rollout dashboard and every license renewal negotiation.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
PHASES = [
    ("pilot", 25),
    ("engineering", 150),
    ("company_wide", 825),
]

def plan_rollout(phases):
    lines = []
    # TODO: keep a running total across phases
    # TODO: append "<name>: <seats> seats (total <cumulative>)" per phase
    return lines

# TODO: print each line returned by plan_rollout(PHASES)
# TODO: print "rollout_complete=<final cumulative total>"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
pilot: 25 seats (total 25)
engineering: 150 seats (total 175)
company_wide: 825 seats (total 1000)
rollout_complete=1000

en: Initialize total = 0 before the loop and do total += seats inside it.
Each line is f"{name}: {seats} seats (total {total})" — append AFTER updating the total.
The final count is sum(s for _, s in PHASES), or just reuse your total after the loop.

practice_challenges.test_code:
out = plan_rollout(PHASES)
assert out == ["pilot: 25 seats (total 25)", "engineering: 150 seats (total 175)", "company_wide: 825 seats (total 1000)"]
assert plan_rollout([]) == []
assert plan_rollout([("solo", 1)]) == ["solo: 1 seats (total 1)"]
assert sum(s for _, s in PHASES) == 1000

q.en: Why is a ChatGPT Enterprise rollout best framed as replacing shadow AI rather than introducing AI?

en: Because ungoverned personal-account usage with company data typically already exists
Because OpenAI requires proof of prior usage
Because shadow AI is always harmless

q.en: Which trio of capabilities defines the enterprise tier of workforce AI chat products across all three vendors?

en: Faster models, more storage, custom fonts
Identity integration (SSO/SCIM), data governance (training opt-out, audit), and admin controls
On-premise hosting, GPU priority, source code access

q.en: What is the strongest argument that 'no training on business data by default' makes for buying enterprise seats?

en: Consumer-tier defaults differ — governed seats close the gap employees are already exposing
It is legally required in all jurisdictions

q.en: What gate should a pilot phase pass before the rollout proceeds to a wider cohort?

en: All employees complete a certification exam
Policy violations are triaged and activation meets its target
The pilot runs for at least a year
